package repository

import (
	"gorm.io/gorm"
)

// Anti-pattern: Repository accepts an HTTP request DTO with json: tags.
// Repositories should accept domain values or repository-specific input types,
// not transport-layer DTOs that carry HTTP binding concerns.

// CreateUserRequest is an HTTP request DTO with json: and binding: tags.
type CreateUserRequest struct {
	Name     string `json:"name" binding:"required"`
	Email    string `json:"email" binding:"required,email"`
	Password string `json:"password" binding:"required,min=8"`
	Role     string `json:"role" binding:"oneof=admin user"`
}

type User struct {
	ID       string `gorm:"column:id"`
	Name     string `gorm:"column:name"`
	Email    string `gorm:"column:email"`
	Password string `gorm:"column:password"`
	Role     string `gorm:"column:role"`
}

type UserRepository struct {
	db *gorm.DB
}

// Create accepts an HTTP request DTO - this is the anti-pattern.
// The repository should not know about json: or binding: tags.
func (r *UserRepository) Create(req *CreateUserRequest) (*User, error) {
	user := User{
		Name:     req.Name,
		Email:    req.Email,
		Password: req.Password,
		Role:     req.Role,
	}
	err := r.db.Create(&user).Error
	return &user, err
}

// Update also accepts the same HTTP DTO.
func (r *UserRepository) Update(id string, req *CreateUserRequest) error {
	return r.db.Model(&User{}).Where("id = ?", id).Updates(User{
		Name:     req.Name,
		Email:    req.Email,
		Password: req.Password,
		Role:     req.Role,
	}).Error
}
